framework: CIS Google Cloud Platform Foundation Benchmark
description: The CIS Google Cloud Platform Foundation Benchmark provides prescriptive guidance for configuring security options for GCP. It covers IAM, logging and monitoring, networking, virtual machines, storage, Cloud SQL, BigQuery, DNS, and KMS.

levels.1: Basic security settings recommended for all GCP projects and organizations. Minimal performance or operational impact.
levels.2: Advanced security settings for high-security environments. May require additional cost, planning, or operational overhead.

sections.title: Identity and Access Management
sections.description: Controls for managing GCP identities, service accounts, API keys, and access permissions using the resource hierarchy-based IAM model.

checks.title: Ensure that Corporate Login Credentials are Used
checks.description: Use corporate Google Workspace or Cloud Identity accounts for GCP access instead of personal Gmail accounts. Corporate accounts enable centralized lifecycle management, password policies, MFA enforcement, and audit logging through the organization's identity provider.

checks.title: Ensure that Multi-Factor Authentication is 'Enabled' for All Non-Service Accounts
checks.description: Enable MFA (2-Step Verification) for all user accounts that access GCP. MFA provides defense against credential compromise from phishing, credential stuffing, and password reuse. Configure through Google Workspace or Cloud Identity admin console.

checks.title: Ensure that Service Account has no Admin Privileges
checks.description: Service accounts should not be granted primitive roles (Owner, Editor) or broad admin roles. Follow the principle of least privilege and assign only the specific predefined or custom roles needed for the service account's function.

checks.title: Ensure that Service Account Keys are Managed and Rotated
checks.description: User-managed service account keys should be rotated every 90 days or less. Prefer Workload Identity Federation or attached service accounts over exported keys. If keys must be used, implement rotation procedures and monitor for key age in Security Command Center.

checks.title: Ensure that IAM Users are Not Assigned the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator Roles at Project Level
checks.description: The Service Account User and Service Account Token Creator roles should be granted on specific service accounts, not at the project level. Project-level grants allow users to impersonate any service account in the project, violating least privilege.

checks.title: Ensure User-Managed/External Keys for Service Accounts are Rotated Every 90 Days or Fewer
checks.description: External service account keys do not expire automatically. Implement a process to rotate these keys every 90 days or less. Use the Organization Policy constraint iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation to prevent key creation and prefer alternatives like Workload Identity.

checks.title: Ensure that Separation of Duties is Enforced while Assigning Service Account Related Roles to Users
checks.description: No single user should have both the Service Account Admin and Service Account User roles. This separation prevents a user from both creating service accounts and using them to access resources, which could enable privilege escalation.

checks.title: Ensure API Keys are Restricted to Only APIs That Application Needs Access
checks.description: API keys should be restricted to only the specific APIs the application needs. Unrestricted API keys can be used to access any enabled API in the project. Also restrict keys by HTTP referrer, IP address, or application (Android/iOS) where possible.

sections.title: Logging and Monitoring
sections.description: Controls for configuring Cloud Audit Logs, log exports, and alerting to ensure visibility into GCP resource activity and security events.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud Audit Logging is Configured Properly Across All Services and All Users from a Project
checks.description: Enable Data Access audit logs for all services and all users. By default, only Admin Activity logs are enabled. Data Access logs capture data read, data write, and metadata read operations which are essential for security monitoring and forensics.

checks.title: Ensure that Sinks are Configured for All Log Entries
checks.description: Configure a log sink to export all log entries (including Admin Activity, Data Access, and System Event logs) to a long-term storage destination such as Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub. Default log retention in Cloud Logging is 30 days for most logs.

checks.title: Ensure Log Metric Filter and Alerts Exist for Project Ownership Assignments/Changes
checks.description: Create a log-based metric and alerting policy to detect changes to project ownership. Project ownership changes grant full control over all resources and should be monitored. Filter: protoPayload.serviceName="cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com" AND ProjectOwnership.

checks.title: Ensure that Logging is Enabled for Cloud Audit Logging
checks.description: Ensure Cloud Audit Logging is not exempted for any users or services. Audit log exemptions create blind spots in security monitoring. Review the auditConfigs in the IAM policy to ensure no exemptedMembers are configured.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud DNS Logging is Enabled for All VPC Networks
checks.description: Enable Cloud DNS logging for all VPC networks to capture DNS queries made by VMs. DNS logs are critical for detecting C2 communications, data exfiltration via DNS tunneling, and resolving suspicious domains.

checks.title: Ensure Log Metric Filter and Alerts Exist for VPC Network Route Changes
checks.description: Create a log-based metric and alerting policy for VPC route changes. Unauthorized route modifications could redirect traffic through attacker-controlled paths. Monitor for compute.routes.insert, compute.routes.patch, and compute.routes.delete operations.

checks.title: Ensure Log Metric Filter and Alerts Exist for Cloud SQL Instance Configuration Changes
checks.description: Create a log-based metric and alerting policy for Cloud SQL instance configuration changes. Database configuration modifications could weaken security (disabling SSL, opening network access). Monitor cloudsql.instances.update operations.

sections.title: Networking
sections.description: Controls for securing GCP networking including VPC configuration, firewall rules, Private Google Access, and load balancer security.

checks.title: Ensure that the Default Network Does Not Exist in a Project
checks.description: Delete the default VPC network from all projects. The default network includes pre-configured firewall rules that allow broad internal communication and SSH/RDP from any source (0.0.0.0/0). Create custom VPCs with explicit, restrictive firewall rules instead.

checks.title: Ensure that SSH Access is Restricted from the Internet
checks.description: Firewall rules should not allow unrestricted SSH access (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0. Use Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) TCP forwarding, OS Login, or VPN for SSH access to instances. The default-allow-ssh rule in the default network should be removed.

checks.title: Ensure that RDP Access is Restricted from the Internet
checks.description: Firewall rules should not allow unrestricted RDP access (port 3389) from 0.0.0.0/0. Use IAP TCP forwarding or VPN for remote desktop access to Windows instances. Restrict source ranges to known trusted IP addresses.

checks.title: Ensure that VPC Flow Logs is Enabled for Every Subnet in a VPC Network
checks.description: Enable VPC Flow Logs on every subnet to capture network flow data. Flow logs record a sample of network flows for traffic monitoring, forensics, and real-time security analysis. Configure appropriate sampling rate and aggregation interval.

checks.title: Ensure No HTTPS or SSL Proxy Load Balancers Permit SSL Policies With Weak Cipher Suites
checks.description: Configure SSL policies on HTTPS and SSL proxy load balancers to use MODERN or RESTRICTED profiles. These profiles exclude weak cipher suites and enforce TLS 1.2+. Avoid the COMPATIBLE profile which includes legacy ciphers for backward compatibility.

checks.title: Ensure Firewall Rules for Instances Behind Identity Aware Proxy (IAP) Only Allow Traffic From Google Cloud Loadbalancer (GCLB) Health Check and Proxy Addresses
checks.description: When using IAP for TCP forwarding, configure firewall rules to only allow traffic from IAP's IP range (35.235.240.0/20). This ensures that instances behind IAP cannot be accessed directly, bypassing IAP's authentication and authorization.

sections.title: Virtual Machines
sections.description: Controls for securing Google Compute Engine instances including OS configuration, disk encryption, serial port access, and metadata security.

checks.title: Ensure that Instances are Not Configured to Use Default Service Accounts
checks.description: Compute Engine instances should not use the default Compute Engine service account. The default service account has the Editor role, providing broad access. Create dedicated service accounts with minimal permissions for each workload.

checks.title: Ensure that Instances are Not Configured to Use Default Service Accounts with Full Access to All Cloud APIs
checks.description: When using the default service account, never grant 'Allow full access to all Cloud APIs' scope. This combined with the default service account's Editor role effectively grants full project access. Use specific scopes or dedicated service accounts.

checks.title: Ensure OS Login Is Enabled for a Project
checks.description: Enable OS Login to manage SSH access using IAM roles instead of managing SSH keys in instance or project metadata. OS Login ties SSH access to Google identity, supports 2FA, and provides centralized access management and audit logging.

checks.title: Ensure 'Enable Connecting to Serial Ports' Is Not Enabled for VM Instance
checks.description: Disable serial port access (enable-serial-port-access metadata) on VM instances. The interactive serial console does not support IP-based access restrictions and could be used for unauthorized access if credentials are compromised.

checks.title: Ensure Compute Instances Are Launched with Shielded VM Enabled
checks.description: Enable Shielded VM features (Secure Boot, vTPM, Integrity Monitoring) on Compute Engine instances. Shielded VMs protect against boot-level and kernel-level malware, rootkits, and firmware attacks through verified boot process integrity.

checks.title: Ensure that Compute Instances Do Not Have Public IP Addresses
checks.description: Compute instances should not have external (public) IP addresses unless absolutely necessary. Use Cloud NAT for outbound internet access and IAP for inbound administrative access. Enforce with the Organization Policy constraint compute.vmExternalIpAccess.

sections.title: Storage
sections.description: Controls for securing Google Cloud Storage buckets including access controls, encryption, and public access prevention.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud Storage Bucket is Not Anonymously or Publicly Accessible
checks.description: Cloud Storage buckets should not grant access to allUsers or allAuthenticatedUsers. Public access to storage buckets is a common source of data leaks. Use the Organization Policy constraint storage.publicAccessPrevention to enforce at the org level.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud Storage Buckets Have Uniform Bucket-Level Access Enabled
checks.description: Enable Uniform bucket-level access to use only IAM for access control, disabling per-object ACLs. This simplifies access management and ensures consistent policy enforcement. Enforce with Organization Policy constraint storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess.

checks.title: Ensure that Retention Policies on Cloud Storage Buckets are configured using Bucket Lock
checks.description: Configure retention policies with Bucket Lock on storage buckets containing compliance-relevant data. Locked retention policies cannot be reduced or removed, ensuring data immutability for the retention period (WORM compliance).

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud Storage Buckets are Encrypted with Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK)
checks.description: Use customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS for Cloud Storage encryption instead of Google-managed keys. CMEK provides control over key lifecycle, rotation, and access policies, and enables key destruction for crypto-shredding.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud Logging Sink Destination Bucket has Retention Policy with Bucket Lock Configured
checks.description: Cloud Storage buckets used as Cloud Logging sink destinations should have retention policies with Bucket Lock enabled. This ensures audit logs cannot be tampered with or deleted before the retention period expires.

sections.title: Cloud SQL Database Services
sections.description: Controls for securing Cloud SQL instances including network access, encryption, backup configuration, and database-specific settings.

checks.title: Ensure that a MySQL Database Instance Does Not Allow Anyone to Connect with Administrative Privileges
checks.description: Restrict the MySQL root user from connecting from any host (%). Configure specific authorized networks or use Cloud SQL Proxy for administrative access. Limit root account access to reduce the blast radius of credential compromise.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud SQL Database Instances Are Not Open to the World
checks.description: Cloud SQL authorized networks should not include 0.0.0.0/0, which allows connections from any IP address. Use specific IP ranges, Private IP, or Cloud SQL Proxy for database connectivity. Public IP with unrestricted access is a critical security risk.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud SQL Database Instances Do Not Implicitly Allow All Public Connections
checks.description: Cloud SQL instances should have authorized networks explicitly configured rather than relying on implicit public access. Prefer Private IP connectivity within VPC over Public IP with authorized networks.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud SQL Database Instances Are Configured with Automated Backups
checks.description: Enable automated backups for all Cloud SQL instances with appropriate retention (7+ days recommended). Automated backups enable point-in-time recovery and protect against data loss from accidental deletion, corruption, or ransomware.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud SQL Database Instances are Configured to Use SSL
checks.description: Require SSL/TLS for all connections to Cloud SQL instances. Configure the 'require SSL' setting to reject unencrypted connections. This protects data in transit between applications and the database from network-level interception.

checks.title: Ensure that Cloud SQL Database Instances are configured with Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK)
checks.description: Use CMEK via Cloud KMS for Cloud SQL instance encryption. This provides control over the encryption key lifecycle and enables compliance with key management requirements. CMEK must be configured at instance creation time.

sections.title: BigQuery
sections.description: Controls for securing BigQuery datasets including access controls, encryption, and data sharing configurations.

checks.title: Ensure that BigQuery Datasets Are Not Anonymously or Publicly Accessible
checks.description: BigQuery datasets should not grant access to allUsers or allAuthenticatedUsers. Public BigQuery datasets expose query results and stored data to anyone on the internet. Review dataset IAM bindings regularly for overly permissive access.

checks.title: Ensure that All BigQuery Tables are Encrypted with Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK)
checks.description: Use CMEK via Cloud KMS for BigQuery table encryption. While BigQuery encrypts all data at rest by default with Google-managed keys, CMEK provides additional control over key rotation, access policies, and the ability to revoke access by disabling or destroying keys.

checks.title: Ensure that a Default Customer-Managed Encryption Key (CMEK) is Specified for Each BigQuery Data Set
checks.description: Configure a default CMEK for each BigQuery dataset so that all new tables inherit the encryption key. This prevents tables from being created with Google-managed encryption when CMEK is required by organizational policy.
